name: System Idle Process
description: A pseudo-process that accounts for idle CPU time on each core. It is not a real image on disk.
security_note: Not a real process; high 'CPU' here simply means the machine is idle, not compromised.

name: System
description: Hosts kernel-mode threads and drivers. Always PID 4 on Windows.
security_note: Kernel-mode rootkits run inside this context; unexpected loaded drivers here are a strong red flag.

name: Registry
description: A minimal process that backs the in-memory registry hives (Windows 10+).
security_note: Legitimate and parented by System; a 'Registry' process with any other parent is suspicious masquerading.

name: smss.exe
description: Session Manager Subsystem: the first user-mode process. Sets up sessions and launches csrss/wininit.
security_note: Must always be a child of System (PID 4). Any other parent indicates process masquerading or injection.

name: csrss.exe
description: Client/Server Runtime Subsystem. Manages console windows and process/thread creation for a session.
security_note: There should be one csrss per session. A csrss.exe running from a non-System32 path is almost always malware.

name: wininit.exe
description: Windows Initialization. Launches services.exe, lsass.exe and lsm in Session 0.
security_note: Parent of the core security stack; anomalies here compromise the whole trust chain.

name: csrss.exe
description: The interactive session's csrss instance (Session 1).
security_note: Legitimate second csrss for the user session; verify the image path is System32.

name: winlogon.exe
description: Handles the secure attention sequence (Ctrl+Alt+Del), loads the user profile and launches the shell.
security_note: Targeted by 'Winlogon Helper DLL' persistence (Shell/Userinit registry keys); audit those values.

name: services.exe
description: The Service Control Manager (SCM). Starts, stops and tracks all Windows services.
security_note: Parent of every svchost; a service pointing at an unusual binary is a classic persistence technique.

name: lsass.exe
description: Local Security Authority Subsystem. Enforces security policy and stores credential material in memory.
security_note: THE prime credential-dumping target (Mimikatz reads its memory). Credential Guard isolates secrets from it.

name: svchost.exe
description: Generic host for Windows services. This instance hosts the DCOM launch group.
security_note: svchost must be a child of services.exe and live in System32. A svchost with a different parent or path is a top malware indicator.

name: svchost.exe
description: Hosts the Remote Procedure Call (RPCSS) service.
security_note: Runs as NETWORK SERVICE by design; the -k switch names the service group it hosts.

name: svchost.exe
description: Hosts many core services (Themes, BITS, Winmgmt/WMI) in the netsvcs group.
security_note: WMI (Winmgmt) lives here and is abused for lateral movement and fileless persistence.

name: spoolsv.exe
description: The Print Spooler service, which manages print jobs.
security_note: Historically vulnerable (PrintNightmare); a spoolsv spawning cmd/powershell is a strong exploitation signal.

name: MsMpEng.exe
description: Microsoft Defender Antimalware Engine, the real-time protection service.
security_note: Attackers try to disable or exclude it; monitor Defender tamper events and service stops.

name: explorer.exe
description: Windows Shell: the desktop, taskbar and file browser. Parent of most user applications.
security_note: Runs at Medium integrity as the user; if explorer spawns powershell/cmd unexpectedly, inspect the command line.

name: chrome.exe
description: The Google Chrome browser broker (main) process.
security_note: The browser broker is Medium integrity; its renderers drop to Low/AppContainer as a sandbox boundary.

name: chrome.exe
description: A sandboxed Chrome renderer process handling untrusted web content.
security_note: Low integrity by design; a renderer that escapes the sandbox is how browser exploits gain a foothold.

name: powershell.exe
description: The Windows PowerShell host, a scripting and automation shell.
security_note: A favourite living-off-the-land tool; watch for -enc/-EncodedCommand, hidden windows and download cradles (Module 10).

name: cmd.exe
description: The classic Windows command interpreter.
security_note: cmd spawned by a server/service process (not explorer) often signals command execution by an attacker.

name: Code.exe
description: Visual Studio Code editor (an Electron application).
security_note: Electron apps spawn many child processes; baseline them so real anomalies stand out.
